channel.title: Data-Quality on Athan Dial - Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content in Data-Quality on Athan Dial - Portfolio

item.title: Data Quality Is an Incident Discipline
item.description:
Context

A single misspelled configuration field quietly pushed six FDA-approved compounds through three discovery programs and wasted about $90, but the real damage was the doubt it sowed in a pipeline that controls six-figure purchasing decisions. The production data platform powering dashboards, nomination tools, and leadership reviews ingested external sources, applied layered transformations, and surfaced boolean flags that dictated procurement. The underlying problem: data quality failures in applied AI workflows rarely explode loudly. They produce plausible numbers that lie quietly until a scientist notices a weird compound on a list months later. Without structured incident response, those errors erode trust faster than they burn dollars.

item.title: From Data Crisis to Data Culture: A Critical Program Incident
item.description:
Context

Mid-2025 was a period of rapid growth at Montai — more programs, more models, more data flowing through pipelines built for smaller scale. Technical debt had accumulated in migration work from earlier systems, creating latent risks that hadn’t yet manifested. Until one critical program.

Facts:

  • Mid-2025: A critical discovery program found predictions missing from the warehouse
  • Impact: Could not evaluate nominations for the affected program (significant program investment at stake)
  • Symptom: Our program monitoring dashboard failed, analysis queries returned incomplete results
  • Urgency: Program decisions on hold, stakeholder trust eroding

The stakes extended beyond the immediate technical bug. This was organizational credibility on the line — scientists needed confidence that data infrastructure wouldn’t become a bottleneck to discovery. A 6-week delay on a critical program signaled deeper quality issues, and stakeholders rightfully questioned whether other datasets harbored similar problems.

item.title: A Biologist Caught What No Automated System Did
item.description:
Context

Two ML model configurations — one trained on a full compound dataset, one trained on a filtered subset — were producing nearly identical predictions. A biologist noticed. There were no automated data quality checks at any stage of the pipeline. No assertions, no monitoring, no alerting. The only reason this failure surfaced at all was that a domain expert recognized a pattern that didn’t make scientific sense.

That’s the starting condition: a failure that was invisible to every system we’d built, caught by someone paying attention.
